hero.sub: ERP sumber terbuka untuk tim konstruksi — estimasi, jadwal, tender, kontrak, tugas lapangan, dan pelaporan dalam satu instalasi. Angka biaya dan waktu tetap sinkron dengan gambar dan BOQ di belakangnya.

final_cta.sub: Instal dengan satu perintah. Jalankan di laptop, VPS, atau cloud pilihan Anda. Self-host selamanya, atau biarkan kami melakukannya.

community.lede: AGPL-3.0 di GitHub. Issues dan roadmap berada di sana; jika Anda ingin melihat apa yang akan datang atau apa yang rusak minggu lalu, semuanya ada di log komit.
community.hero_title: Star the repo, shape the roadmap.
community.hero_desc: Setiap issue, PR, dan item roadmap bersifat publik di GitHub. Buka issue, beri komentar pada hal yang penting, atau kirim PR — kontribusi dengan ukuran apa pun diterima.
community.modcard_1_desc: Memulai, SDK modul, referensi API, dan panduan penerapan.
community.modcard_2_desc: Setiap bug, permintaan fitur, dan item roadmap dilacak secara publik.
community.modcard_3_desc: Templat cookiecutter, SDK terketik, OpenAPI auto-gen. Kirim plugin dalam sore hari.
community.modcard_4_desc: Questions, ideas and show-and-tell. Hop in for things that aren’t quite an issue yet.

numbers.lede: Numbers from the current release. Some of these will be out of date by the time you read this — the repo moves fast.
numbers.card1_label: item biaya siap pakai di seluruh kelompok kerja dan paket pekerjaan.
numbers.card2_label: Ingest CAD asli — DWG, RVT, IFC, dan DGN ke dalam satu format kanonis.
numbers.card3_label: Modul plug-in yang mencakup alur kerja lapangan lengkap — proyek, BOQ, takeoff, tender, dan lainnya.
numbers.card4_label: Inti sumber terbuka di bawah AGPL-3.0. Tanpa vendor lock-in. Data Anda tetap milik Anda.

tour.lede: Seven steps from first-run to finished tender. Follow them in order or jump ahead — each one plays a short capture from the running app.
tour.info_desc_default: First run. Pick language, region and currency — the app comes with a starter cost database (55,000+ priced positions) already loaded. Zero setup, no seed-data dance.

steps.caption: 01 · onboarding
steps.title: Onboarding
steps.desc: First run. Pick language, region and currency — the app comes with a starter cost database (55,000+ priced positions) already loaded. Zero setup, no seed-data dance.

steps.caption: 02 · create a project
steps.title: Buat proyek
steps.desc: Name it, set the location, invite the team. Every project gets its own dashboard, document room, validation rules and a set of estimates. Switch between projects in one click.

steps.caption: 03 · start the estimate
steps.title: Mulai estimasi
steps.desc: Open a new BOQ. Pick a classification tree — DIN 276, NRM, MasterFormat, or your own — and start building sections. Drag to reorder, nest any depth, copy from templates.

steps.caption: 04 · add positions
steps.title: Tambah posisi
steps.desc: Search 55,000+ priced positions by trade, region or keyword. Drop them straight into the BOQ with their unit rates already filled in. Typeahead, semantic search, instant totals.

steps.caption: 05 · resources & assemblies
steps.title: Resources & assemblies
steps.desc: Each position holds its resource breakdown — materials, labour, equipment — grouped as assemblies. Override rates, apply regional factors, watch totals recalculate across the project.

steps.caption: 06 · link BIM
steps.title: Link the BIM model
steps.desc: Drop an IFC or RVT file. Select walls, slabs, openings — push quantities straight into the BOQ. Re-link on model revision; the positions keep their rates and comments.

steps.caption: 07 · link CAD / PDF
steps.title: Link CAD & PDF drawings
steps.desc: DWG and DGN with layer-aware selection. PDF with click-to-measure and AI pre-fill. Bulk-link groups — dozens of elements into positions in one pass.

steps.caption: 08 · validate
steps.title: Validate the estimate
steps.desc: Traffic-light pass across the whole BOQ. Built-in rule sets for DIN 276, NRM, MasterFormat and GAEB. Plus quality rules — zero prices, missing quantities, outlier rates — flagged inline.

steps.caption: 09 · tasks & follow-ups
steps.title: Tasks & follow-ups
steps.desc: Every flagged row, BIM element or reviewer comment can become a task. Kanban board per project, assignees, due dates, status columns — the estimators' to-do list lives where the estimate lives.

steps.caption: 10 · export the tender
steps.title: Export the tender
steps.desc: Totals roll up per section, trade and discipline. Export GAEB X83/X84/X89, Excel, PDF or JSON. Send the tender pack to subcontractors, collect bids, compare side-by-side.

compare.lede: Honest table. Commercial suites are genuinely better at a few things — mostly integrations paid for over a decade. Everywhere else, the gap is smaller than sales teams claim.
compare.row_55k: 55,000+ cost items out of the box
compare.note: ✓ fully supported  ·  ~ partial or via plugin  ·  ✗ not supported

video_intro.title: See it work. In one take.
video_intro.sub: A short walkthrough of the whole pipeline — drawing in, tender out. No voiceover, no narration, just the product doing what it does.

module_lab.title:
Add a module. Remove one.
No rebuild.
module_lab.sub: Every feature is a self-contained package. Drop it in, pull it out — the running install picks it up in seconds, zero downtime.

developers.lede: Every feature is a Python package with a manifest. You can add one to a running install without rebuilding anything. The APIs are typed; the OpenAPI spec is generated, not hand-maintained.
developers.bullet1_text: Module SDK — scaffold one with make module-new
developers.bullet2: <svg viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2.4" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"><polyline points="20 6 9 17 4 12"/></svg>Typed REST API, auto-generated OpenAPI spec
developers.bullet3: <svg viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2.4" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"><polyline points="20 6 9 17 4 12"/></svg>Hooks & event bus — extend without forking

voices.para1: Over the past ten years, I have been deeply involved in resource management for construction projects. This journey inevitably led me to study the history of the technologies that have shaped the industry—from the earliest attempts at design automation to modern ERP platforms (see the series of articles “The Lobbyists’ Wars and the Development of BIM” and “The History of the BIM Map”). Without understanding where we came from, it is impossible to see where we are going.
voices.para2: Over the years, dozens of articles have come off my desk, read by millions of professionals around the world. At the same time, I’ve consulted with major construction and consulting firms, developers, and software vendors themselves on data management in projects—helping them navigate processes where data is not a byproduct but the foundation for decision-making. This work gave me a rare opportunity to see the industry from both sides: through the eyes of those who create the tools and through the eyes of those who use them in real projects every day. Many of these observations and reflections are collected in my book Data-Driven Construction, which is now available in 16 languages—datadrivenconstruction.io/books.
voices.para3: Two other things have kept me busy: helping non-developers get into proprietary formats that were never meant to be opened, and finding a cleaner way to describe construction work through a resource model. These efforts have resulted in free tools—DDC CAD/BIM data converters (Revit, IFC, DWG, DGN → structured data), available on GitHub, and the multilingual CWICR database of construction works and resources—over 55,000 items in 11 languages, published as OpenConstructionEstimate-DDC-CWICR. All of this was a necessary step toward an idea I’ve been pursuing for the past decade — an open-source modular ERP for the construction industry.
voices.changelog_more: Full changelog on GitHub <svg viewBox="0 0 24 24" width="12" height="12" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"><path d="M7 7h10v10"/><path d="M7 17 17 7"/></svg>
voices.para4: The recent generation of AI tooling finally made it feasible to consolidate that work — methodology, data models, and prior implementations — into a single platform. It’s now public and open source.
voices.pull: Progress is born from dialogue—from the clash of perspectives and openness to new approaches. I would be grateful if you would be willing to participate in this conversation on the inevitable Uberization of the construction industry and the transparency of cost and time estimation processes for construction projects.

pricing.lede: Two paths. Run it yourself for free under AGPL-3.0, or talk to us when you need hosted support, a commercial license for the converters, or anything else that shouldn't live in the open-source license.
pricing.tier1_desc: The full open-source platform. Run it on your infra, no strings attached.
pricing.tier1_feature4: All 18 modules out of the box
pricing.tier2_desc: When you need managed hosting, commercial licensing for the converters, or anything that doesn't fit under AGPL — tell us what you need and we'll scope it.
pricing.tier2_feature1: Managed cloud hosting — we run it for you
pricing.tier2_feature2: Commercial license for CAD converters & cost DB
pricing.tier2_feature6: Direct line for onboarding and roadmap requests

license.lede: The core is AGPL-3.0 — great for self-hosting, not always a fit for proprietary or embedded products. A commercial license lets you ship OpenConstructionERP inside a closed-source product, on customer infrastructure or as part of a paid SaaS — with a signed agreement, invoicing and support.
license.reassure2: OEM, embedding and closed-source use covered
license.reassure3: SLA, priority support and success manager
license.use_internal: Internal use only (AGPL may be fine)
license.use_embedded: Embedded in our own commercial software
license.field_message: Anything you'd like us to know
license.field_message_placeholder: Deployment scale, timeline, specific compliance needs…
license.consent: I agree that OpenConstructionERP / DataDrivenConstruction may contact me about this inquiry, store the submitted data for the purpose of responding, and use my email to send occasional product news and updates. You can unsubscribe at any time.

faq.q1: Is the core really free and open-source?
faq.a1: Yes. The core is AGPL-3.0 on GitHub. You can self-host forever, audit every line, fork it, or run it in production. We also offer a commercial license for enterprises that can't use AGPL.
faq.q2: What hardware do I need to run it?
faq.a2: The core runs on a 2 GB VPS. One PostgreSQL instance is all you need for a small team. AI features (takeoff, vector search) are optional — features degrade gracefully when AI services aren't available.
faq.q3: How do I import from my existing system?
faq.a3: Native GAEB XML (X83 / X84 / X89), Excel and CSV import are built in. Connectors for proprietary systems ship as modules — the marketplace already has a handful, including workflow integration via n8n. Custom migrations are available via Enterprise support.
faq.q4: Do I need AI services to use the product?
faq.a4: No. The BOQ editor, validation pipeline, CAD ingest and cost database all work without AI. AI takeoff and semantic search are optional enhancements — you can bring your own model or use the hosted CV/LLM services via Pro.
faq.a5: Built by DataDrivenConstruction — the team behind CWICR (the 55,000-item cost database shipped here), cad2data, and 10+ years of construction-tech engineering. We ship our own tools on it.
faq.a6: Absolutely. The SDK ships with a cookiecutter template — scaffold a module in one command, drop it in the marketplace when it's ready. Good candidates: regional cost databases, ERP connectors, custom validation rule sets.
faq.q7: Where is my data stored? Does anything leave my server?
faq.a7: On your infrastructure. Projects, drawings and cost data live in your PostgreSQL and your object storage — nothing is phoned home, no telemetry by default. If you enable hosted AI services, only the payload you send is processed; it's opt-in per workspace and you can point the client at self-hosted models instead.
faq.q8: What CAD and BIM formats are supported?
faq.a8: DWG, DGN, RVT and IFC ingest through the cad2data pipeline into a canonical format — one schema to query, classify and cost against. PDF drawings go through the takeoff pipeline (vector extraction + OCR). New formats land as modules, not core rewrites.
faq.q9: How does the commercial license work?
faq.a9: AGPL-3.0 is fine for most internal and service use. If you embed the product in a closed SaaS, ship it inside a proprietary application, or need an explicit waiver for procurement, talk to us — commercial terms cover that, plus priority support and enterprise SLA. No per-seat metering, no forced cloud.
faq.a10: Yes, and we mean it. Everything you put in comes back out — GAEB XML, Excel, CSV, JSON, Parquet, or a direct PostgreSQL dump. The schema is documented, the source is open; no proprietary file formats, no escrow games, no hostage data.
faq.q11: How often do you ship updates?
faq.a11: Minor releases roughly every 2–3 weeks, patch releases as needed. Changelog lives on GitHub, every tagged version has a Docker image, and migrations are reversible. You decide when to upgrade — self-hosted means nobody forces a deploy window on you.
faq.q12: Which languages and regions are supported?
faq.a12: 30 languages ship in the core i18n. Cost database labels, validation rule sets and classification trees are per-region modules — pick the ones you need, add your own. No hard-coded currency, no hard-coded date format.

footer.tagline: Open-source construction ERP. AI-powered BOQ, multi-CAD ingest, 55,000+ cost items — all in one lightweight install.

newsletter.title: Get product updates straight in your inbox.
